## Assignment Overview

In this lab, you will apply the spatial predictive modeling techniques demonstrated 
in the class exercise using a different 311 service request type of your choice as the predictor variable. You will build a complete spatial predictive model, document your process, and interpret your results.

### Learning Objectives

By completing this assignment, you will demonstrate your ability to:

- Adapt example code to analyze a new dataset
- Build spatial features for predictive modeling
- Apply count regression techniques to spatial data
- Implement spatial cross-validation
- Interpret and communicate model results
- Critically evaluate model performance

---

## Step 1: Choose Your 311 Violation Type

### Getting the Data

Visit the Chicago 311 Service Requests dataset:

<https://data.cityofchicago.org/stories/s/311-Dataset-Changes-12-11-2018/d7nq-5g7t>

Browse the available service request types (e.g., Graffiti Removal, Pothole Repair, Street Light Out, Sanitation Code Violations, etc.) and **choose one violation type** that interests you.

### Requirements for Your Choice

- Choose a **different** violation type than the abandoned cars we did in class.
---

## Step 2: Complete the Analysis

Using the class exercise as your template, adapt the code to analyze your chosen 311 violation type.

### What to Include

Work through all major sections of the analysis:

#### Part 1: Data Loading & Exploration
### Part 1: Data Loading and Exploration

In this first section, I loaded the main spatial datasets needed for the analysis: the Chicago boundary, police districts, 2017 burglary data, and my chosen 311 request type, graffiti removal complaints. I chose graffiti complaints because they are a visible form of neighborhood disorder and there are enough records in the dataset to analyze spatial patterns across the city.

This step is important because before building any model, I need to understand what the raw data looks like and where the observations are located. Mapping the burglary points and graffiti complaints helps show whether the data is evenly distributed or spatially clustered. This matters because if the 311 complaints are clustered in certain neighborhoods, they may help explain spatial variation in burglary counts later in the model.

From the maps, I found that graffiti complaints are not randomly or evenly spread across Chicago. They are concentrated in specific parts of the city, especially around central, north, and west-side areas, while other areas have fewer complaints. This suggests that graffiti complaints have a clear spatial pattern, which makes them a reasonable predictor to use in the rest of the analysis.

- Load your 311 data and Chicago spatial boundaries


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- PART 1: DATA LOADING & EXPLORATION ----

# Load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(here)
library(viridis)
library(patchwork)
library(janitor)

options(scipen = 999)

# Simple clean map theme
theme_lab <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray30"),
      axis.text = element_blank(),
      axis.title = element_blank(),
      panel.grid = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
}

theme_set(theme_lab())

# ---- Load Chicago boundary ----
chicagoBoundary <- 
  st_read("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/urbanSpatial/Public-Policy-Analytics-Landing/master/DATA/Chapter5/chicagoBoundary.geojson") %>%
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")
```

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- Load 311 Graffiti Removal data ----
# Historical dataset ID: hec5-y4x5
# This keeps only 2017 requests with coordinates

graffiti_311 <- read_csv(
  "https://data.cityofchicago.org/resource/hec5-y4x5.csv?$limit=50000"
) %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  mutate(
    creation_date = as.Date(creation_date)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    year(creation_date) == 2017,
    !is.na(latitude),
    !is.na(longitude)
  ) %>%
  st_as_sf(
    coords = c("longitude", "latitude"),
    crs = 4326
  ) %>%
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")

# ---- Basic summaries ----
cat("Chicago boundary loaded:", nrow(chicagoBoundary), "feature(s)\n")
```

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- Load 2017 Graffiti Removal 311 data correctly ----

library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(lubridate)
library(janitor)
library(viridis)
library(patchwork)

graffiti_url <- paste0(
  "https://data.cityofchicago.org/resource/hec5-y4x5.csv?",
  "$limit=500000&",
  "$where=creation_date between '2017-01-01T00:00:00' and '2017-12-31T23:59:59'"
)

graffiti_311 <- read_csv(URLencode(graffiti_url), show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  filter(!is.na(latitude), !is.na(longitude)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")

cat("Graffiti 311 requests loaded:", nrow(graffiti_311), "requests\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Graffiti 311 requests loaded: 112698 requests
```


:::
:::


- Create visualizations showing the spatial distribution of your violation type


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_sf(data = graffiti_311, color = "#2a9d8f", size = 0.08, alpha = 0.25) +
  labs(
    title = "311 Graffiti Requests",
    subtitle = paste0("Chicago, 2017 | n = ", nrow(graffiti_311))
  ) +
  theme_lab()

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_density_2d_filled(
    data = data.frame(st_coordinates(graffiti_311)),
    aes(X, Y),
    alpha = 0.75,
    bins = 8
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(
    option = "plasma",
    direction = -1,
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Graffiti Density",
    subtitle = "Kernel density visualization"
  ) +
  theme_lab()

p1 + p2 +
  plot_annotation(title = "Spatial Distribution of Graffiti 311 Requests")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


- Describe patterns you observe

The graffiti removal requests are clearly not evenly spread across the city. Instead, they are heavily concentrated in certain areas, especially in central and parts of the north and west sides of Chicago. There are noticeable clusters where requests are very dense, while other areas, particularly farther south or on the edges of the city, have relatively few requests. This suggests that graffiti activity is spatially clustered rather than random, and that some neighborhoods experience much higher levels of reported graffiti than others.

#### Part 2: Fishnet Grid Creation

In this step, I created a 500m by 500m fishnet grid that covers the entire Chicago boundary. Instead of working with individual point data, I divided the city into evenly sized grid cells and then counted how many graffiti complaints fall inside each cell. This converts the data from point-level observations into area-based counts.

This step is important because the regression models I use later require a consistent unit of analysis. Individual points are difficult to model directly, especially when trying to compare across space. By aggregating the data into grid cells, I can treat each cell as a comparable observation and analyze how counts vary across different parts of the city.

From the results, I found that the distribution of graffiti complaints across grid cells is highly uneven. Most cells have relatively few complaints, while a small number of cells have very high counts. The fishnet map also shows clear clusters where complaints are concentrated, rather than being spread uniformly. This reinforces the idea that graffiti activity is spatially clustered, which justifies using spatial features and count-based models in the next steps.

- Create a 500m x 500m fishnet grid

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- PART 2: CREATE 500m x 500m FISHNET GRID ----

fishnet <- st_make_grid(
  chicagoBoundary,
  cellsize = 500,
  square = TRUE
) %>%
  st_sf() %>%
  mutate(uniqueID = row_number()) %>%
  st_transform("ESRI:102271")

# Keep only grid cells that intersect Chicago
fishnet <- fishnet[chicagoBoundary, ]

# Check grid
cat("Fishnet grid created\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Fishnet grid created
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}

```


```{.r .cell-code}
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, fill = NA, color = "gray70", linewidth = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "500m x 500m Fishnet Grid",

  ) +

```

- Aggregate your violations to grid cells

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- AGGREGATE GRAFFITI REQUESTS TO GRID CELLS ----

graffiti_fishnet <- st_join(
  graffiti_311,
  fishnet,
  join = st_within
) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(uniqueID) %>%
  summarize(graffiti_count = n())

# Join counts back to the fishnet
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  left_join(graffiti_fishnet, by = "uniqueID") %>%
  mutate(graffiti_count = replace_na(graffiti_count, 0))

# Check distribution
summary(fishnet$graffiti_count)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   0.00    1.00    8.00   45.83   52.00 1281.00 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("Cells with zero graffiti requests:",
    sum(fishnet$graffiti_count == 0),
    "out of",
    nrow(fishnet),
    "\n")
```

```
Cells with zero graffiti requests: 579 out of 2458 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
  st_drop_geometry()

# Check summary first
summary(fishnet_counts$graffiti_count)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Histogram zoomed to the 95th percentile
ggplot(fishnet_counts, aes(x = graffiti_count)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 40,
    fill = "#2a9d8f",
    color = "white"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, quantile(fishnet_counts$graffiti_count, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Graffiti Requests per Grid Cell",
    subtitle = "Zoomed to the 95th percentile because a few cells have very high counts",
    x = "Graffiti Requests per Grid Cell",
    y = "Number of Grid Cells"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{.r .cell-code}
    bins = 40,
```

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = graffiti_count), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Graffiti Requests",
    option = "plasma",
    trans = "sqrt"
  ) +
```

![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


#### Part 3: Spatial Features

### Part 3: Creating Spatial Features

In this step, I created several spatial features that capture how each grid cell relates to nearby graffiti activity. First, I calculated a k-nearest neighbor measure, which gives the average distance from each grid cell to the closest graffiti complaints. Then, I ran a Local Moran’s I analysis to identify statistically significant clusters of high and low graffiti activity, which allowed me to label certain areas as hot spots or cold spots. Finally, I created a distance-to-hotspot variable, which measures how far each grid cell is from the nearest high-activity cluster.

This step is important because the raw count of graffiti complaints alone does not fully capture the spatial relationships in the data. Locations that are near clusters of activity may behave differently than isolated areas, even if their raw counts are similar. By adding these spatial features, I can give the model more information about the surrounding environment of each grid cell, which should improve its ability to explain variation in the data.

From the results, I found clear evidence of spatial clustering. The Local Moran’s I analysis identified several high-high clusters, meaning areas with high graffiti activity surrounded by other high-activity areas. There were very few cold spots, suggesting that low-activity areas are more dispersed. The distance-based features also show meaningful patterns, where areas closer to existing graffiti activity tend to have higher counts. Overall, this step confirmed that spatial dependence is a key characteristic of the data and should be incorporated into the modeling.


- Calculate k-nearest neighbor features
- Perform Local Moran's I analysis
- Identify hot spots and cold spots
- Create distance-to-hotspot measures
- Join any additional contextual data if you are looking for more to do and really get into this (e.g., demographics, land use)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- PART 3: SPATIAL FEATURES ----

library(spdep)
library(FNN)

# Make sure fishnet has centroids
fishnet_centroids <- st_centroid(fishnet)

# ============================================================
# 1. K-NEAREST NEIGHBOR FEATURE
# ============================================================

# Coordinates of grid cell centroids
fishnet_coords <- st_coordinates(fishnet_centroids)

# Coordinates of graffiti request points
graffiti_coords <- st_coordinates(graffiti_311)

# Calculate mean distance from each grid cell to the 3 nearest graffiti requests
nn_result <- get.knnx(
  data = graffiti_coords,
  query = fishnet_coords,
  k = 3
)

fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    graffiti_nn = rowMeans(nn_result$nn.dist)
  )

summary(fishnet$graffiti_nn)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
   2.496   68.656  155.394  275.758  340.075 2358.802 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Visualize nearest neighbor distance
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = graffiti_nn), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Mean Distance",
    option = "magma"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distance to 3 Nearest Graffiti Requests",
    subtitle = "Lower values indicate closer proximity to graffiti activity"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ============================================================
# 2. LOCAL MORAN'S I ANALYSIS
# ============================================================

calculate_local_morans <- function(data, variable, k = 5) {
  
  coords <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(data))
  
  neighbors <- knn2nb(knearneigh(coords, k = k))
  weights <- nb2listw(neighbors, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
  
  local_moran <- localmoran(data[[variable]], weights, zero.policy = TRUE)
  
  mean_val <- mean(data[[variable]], na.rm = TRUE)
  
  data %>%
    mutate(
      local_i = local_moran[, 1],
      p_value = local_moran[, 5],
      is_significant = p_value < 0.05,
      moran_class = case_when(
        !is_significant ~ "Not Significant",
        local_i > 0 & .data[[variable]] > mean_val ~ "High-High",
        local_i > 0 & .data[[variable]] <= mean_val ~ "Low-Low",
        local_i < 0 & .data[[variable]] > mean_val ~ "High-Low",
        local_i < 0 & .data[[variable]] <= mean_val ~ "Low-High",
        TRUE ~ "Not Significant"
      )
    )
}

fishnet <- calculate_local_morans(
  data = fishnet,
  variable = "graffiti_count",
  k = 5
)

table(fishnet$moran_class)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

      High-High        Low-High Not Significant 
            276              18            2164 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Visualize Local Moran's I clusters
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = moran_class), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "High-High" = "#d7191c",
      "High-Low" = "#fdae61",
      "Low-High" = "#abd9e9",
      "Low-Low" = "#2c7bb6",
      "Not Significant" = "gray90"
    ),
    name = "Cluster Type"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Local Moran's I: Graffiti Complaint Clusters",
    subtitle = "High-High cells are graffiti hot spots; Low-Low cells are cold spots"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-2.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ============================================================
# 3. IDENTIFY HOT SPOTS AND COLD SPOTS
# ============================================================

hotspots <- fishnet %>%
  filter(moran_class == "High-High") %>%
  st_centroid()

coldspots <- fishnet %>%
  filter(moran_class == "Low-Low") %>%
  st_centroid()

cat("Number of graffiti hot spot cells:", nrow(hotspots), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Number of graffiti hot spot cells: 276 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("Number of graffiti cold spot cells:", nrow(coldspots), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Number of graffiti cold spot cells: 0 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ============================================================
# 4. DISTANCE TO HOT SPOTS
# ============================================================

if (nrow(hotspots) > 0) {
  
  fishnet <- fishnet %>%
    mutate(
      dist_to_graffiti_hotspot = as.numeric(
        st_distance(st_centroid(fishnet), st_union(hotspots))
      )
    )
  
} else {
  
  fishnet <- fishnet %>%
    mutate(dist_to_graffiti_hotspot = NA_real_)
  
  cat("No significant graffiti hot spots found.\n")
}

summary(fishnet$dist_to_graffiti_hotspot)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
      0    1118    3905    5441    8732   20863 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Visualize distance to graffiti hot spots
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = dist_to_graffiti_hotspot), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Distance",
    option = "plasma"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distance to Nearest Graffiti Hot Spot",
    subtitle = "Distance from each grid cell to the nearest High-High graffiti cluster"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-3.png){width=672}
:::
:::


#### Part 4: Count Regression Models


In this step, I fit two different count regression models to explain variation in graffiti complaints across grid cells: a Poisson model and a Negative Binomial model. Both models use the spatial features I created earlier, like distance to nearby graffiti and distance to hotspots, to predict the number of complaints in each cell.

This step is important because the outcome I am trying to model is a count variable, which means standard linear regression is not appropriate. The Poisson model is the simplest option for count data, but it assumes that the mean and variance are equal. Based on the earlier distribution plots, I already expected that this assumption would not hold, since the data is highly skewed with some extreme values. The Negative Binomial model relaxes this assumption and is better suited for overdispersed data.

From the results, I found strong evidence of overdispersion, which makes the Poisson model a poor fit. This is also reflected in the AIC comparison, where the Negative Binomial model has a much lower AIC than the Poisson model, indicating a significantly better fit. The coefficients in the Negative Binomial model are also statistically significant and negative, meaning that as distance from graffiti activity or hotspots increases, the expected number of complaints decreases. Overall, the Negative Binomial model is clearly more appropriate for this data and will be the model I rely on moving forward.


- Fit Poisson regression
- Fit Negative Binomial regression
- Compare model fit (AIC)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- PART 4: COUNT REGRESSION MODELS ----

library(MASS)
library(broom)
library(kableExtra)

# Create clean modeling dataset
fishnet_model <- fishnet %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::select(
    uniqueID,
    graffiti_count,
    graffiti_nn,
    dist_to_graffiti_hotspot
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(graffiti_count),
    !is.na(graffiti_nn),
    !is.na(dist_to_graffiti_hotspot)
  )

# Check modeling data
summary(fishnet_model)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    uniqueID    graffiti_count     graffiti_nn       dist_to_graffiti_hotspot
 Min.   :  37   Min.   :   0.00   Min.   :   2.496   Min.   :    0           
 1st Qu.:1137   1st Qu.:   1.00   1st Qu.:  68.656   1st Qu.: 1118

 3rd Qu.:3263   3rd Qu.:  52.00   3rd Qu.: 340.075   3rd Qu.: 8732           
 Max.   :4428   Max.   :1281.00   Max.   :2358.802   Max.   :20863           
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- Fit Poisson Regression ----

poisson_model <- glm(
  graffiti_count ~ graffiti_nn + dist_to_graffiti_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model,
  family = "poisson"
)

summary(poisson_model)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- Check Overdispersion ----

dispersion <- sum(residuals(poisson_model, type = "pearson")^2) /
  poisson_model$df.residual

cat("Poisson dispersion parameter:", round(dispersion, 2), "\n")
```


```{.r .cell-code}
  cat("Overdispersion detected. Negative Binomial model is likely better.\n")
} else {
  cat("Poisson dispersion looks acceptable.\n")
}
```



:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- Fit Negative Binomial Regression ----

nb_model <- glm.nb(
  graffiti_count ~ graffiti_nn + dist_to_graffiti_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model
)

summary(nb_model)
```

#### Part 5: Spatial Cross-Validation (2017)

In this step, I implemented a spatial cross-validation approach using Leave-One-Group-Out (LOGO) cross-validation, where each police district is treated as a separate group. For each iteration, I trained the Negative Binomial model on all districts except one, and then tested the model on the held-out district. I then calculated error metrics like MAE and RMSE for each fold to evaluate how well the model performs on unseen spatial areas.

This step is important because standard cross-validation methods randomly split the data, which does not work well for spatial data. Nearby grid cells are often very similar, so randomly splitting them can lead to overly optimistic performance estimates. By leaving out entire districts, I am forcing the model to predict in completely new geographic areas, which is a much more realistic test of how it would perform in practice.

From the results, I found that the model’s performance varies across districts. Some districts have relatively low error, meaning the model generalizes well there, while others have much higher error, suggesting that the model struggles in those areas. The average MAE and RMSE give an overall sense of performance, but the variation across districts is just as important. This suggests that while the model captures general spatial patterns, it does not perform equally well everywhere, which is an important limitation to keep in mind.


- Implement Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation on 2017 data
- Calculate and report error metrics (MAE, RMSE)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- PART 5: SPATIAL CROSS-VALIDATION (2017) ----

library(MASS)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Join police districts to fishnet for spatial cross-validation
fishnet_cv <- st_join(
  fishnet,
  policeDistricts,
  join = st_within,
  left = TRUE
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(District))

# Create clean modeling dataset with district labels
fishnet_cv_model <- fishnet_cv %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::select(
    uniqueID,
    District,
    graffiti_count,
    graffiti_nn,
    dist_to_graffiti_hotspot
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(District),
    !is.na(graffiti_count),
    !is.na(graffiti_nn),
    !is.na(dist_to_graffiti_hotspot)
  )

# Check districts
cat("Number of districts:", length(unique(fishnet_cv_model$District)), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Number of districts: 22 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cat("Number of grid cells:", nrow(fishnet_cv_model), "\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Number of grid cells: 1708 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Get unique districts
districts <- unique(fishnet_cv_model$District)

# Empty table to store results
cv_results <- tibble()

cat("Running Leave-One-Group-Out spatial cross-validation...\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Running Leave-One-Group-Out spatial cross-validation...
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
for (i in seq_along(districts)) {
  
  test_district <- districts[i]
  
  # Split data by district
  train_data <- fishnet_cv_model %>%
    filter(District != test_district)
  
  test_data <- fishnet_cv_model %>%
    filter(District == test_district)
  
  # Fit Negative Binomial model on all districts except the test district
  cv_model <- glm.nb(
    graffiti_count ~ graffiti_nn + dist_to_graffiti_hotspot,
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # Predict on the held-out district
  test_predictions <- test_data %>%
    mutate(
      prediction = predict(cv_model, newdata = test_data, type = "response"),
      error = graffiti_count - prediction,
      abs_error = abs(error),
      squared_error = error^2
    )
  
  # Calculate error metrics
  mae <- mean(test_predictions$abs_error, na.rm = TRUE)
  rmse <- sqrt(mean(test_predictions$squared_error, na.rm = TRUE))
  
  # Store fold results
  cv_results <- bind_rows(
    cv_results,
    tibble(
      fold = i,
      test_district = test_district,
      n_test = nrow(test_data),
      mae = mae,
      rmse = rmse
    )
  )
  
  cat(
    "Fold", i, "of", length(districts),
    "- District", test_district,
    "- MAE:", round(mae, 2),
    "- RMSE:", round(rmse, 2), "\n"
  )
}
```

#### Part 6: Model Evaluation

In this step, I compared the performance of my Negative Binomial model to a simpler baseline approach using Kernel Density Estimation (KDE). The KDE method essentially smooths past graffiti locations to predict where complaints are likely to occur, without using any additional predictors. I generated KDE-based predictions for each grid cell and then compared those to the predictions from my model using error metrics like MAE and RMSE, as well as visual maps.

This step is important because it helps determine whether the added complexity of the model is actually useful. If a simple smoothing approach like KDE performs just as well or better, then the regression model may not be adding much value. This comparison forces me to justify the model rather than assuming it is better just because it is more complex.

From the results, I found that the KDE baseline captures the overall spatial pattern of graffiti complaints quite well, especially the major hotspots, but it is very smooth and misses a lot of local variation. The Negative Binomial model, on the other hand, is able to capture more fine-grained differences across grid cells, even if it is still imperfect. In terms of metrics, the two approaches perform fairly similarly, with the model slightly improving on MAE while the KDE sometimes performs better on RMSE. Overall, the model provides some additional value by incorporating spatial features, but the improvement over the KDE baseline is relatively modest.

- Compare to KDE baseline


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- PART 6: MODEL EVALUATION: COMPARE TO KDE BASELINE ----
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6

library(spatstat.geom)
library(spatstat.explore)
library(terra)
library(MASS)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(patchwork)

# ---- 1. Create KDE baseline from graffiti point locations ----

graffiti_ppp <- as.ppp(
  st_coordinates(graffiti_311),
  W = as.owin(st_bbox(chicagoBoundary))
)

kde_graffiti <- density.ppp(
  graffiti_ppp,
  sigma = 1000,
  edge = TRUE
)

kde_raster <- rast(kde_graffiti)

fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    kde_value = terra::extract(
      kde_raster,
      vect(fishnet),
      fun = mean,
      na.rm = TRUE
    )[, 2]
  )

# ---- 2. Fit final Negative Binomial model ----

final_nb_model <- glm.nb(
  graffiti_count ~ graffiti_nn + dist_to_graffiti_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model
)

fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    prediction_nb = predict(
      final_nb_model,
      newdata = fishnet %>% st_drop_geometry(),
      type = "response"
    )
  )

# ---- 3. Convert KDE values into predicted counts ----

kde_sum <- sum(fishnet$kde_value, na.rm = TRUE)
count_sum <- sum(fishnet$graffiti_count, na.rm = TRUE)

fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    prediction_kde = (kde_value / kde_sum) * count_sum
  )

# ---- 4. Readable comparison maps ----

map_theme <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text = element_blank(),
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 9),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8)
  )

p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = graffiti_count), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "Actual",
    option = "plasma",
    trans = "sqrt"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Actual Counts") +
  map_theme

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_nb), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "NB Prediction",
    option = "plasma",
    trans = "sqrt"
  ) +
  labs(title = "Negative Binomial") +
  map_theme

p3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_kde), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicagoBoundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "KDE Prediction",
    option = "plasma",
    trans = "sqrt"
  ) +
  labs(title = "KDE Baseline") +
  map_theme

(p1 | p2 | p3) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Actual vs. Predicted Graffiti Complaint Counts",
    subtitle = "Comparing the Negative Binomial model to the KDE baseline"
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](lab_4_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# ---- 5. Calculate performance metrics ----

model_comparison_metrics <- fishnet %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(graffiti_count),
    !is.na(prediction_nb),
    !is.na(prediction_kde)
  ) %>%
  summarize(
    nb_mae = mean(abs(graffiti_count - prediction_nb)),
    nb_rmse = sqrt(mean((graffiti_count - prediction_nb)^2)),
    kde_mae = mean(abs(graffiti_count - prediction_kde)),
    kde_rmse = sqrt(mean((graffiti_count - prediction_kde)^2))
  )

model_comparison_metrics %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "metric",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  separate(metric, into = c("model", "metric"), sep = "_") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = value) %>%
  mutate(
    mae = round(mae, 2),
    rmse = round(rmse, 2)
  ) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Model Performance Comparison: Negative Binomial vs. KDE Baseline"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
```
